coredata.dc:title: Rapid response and highly selective sensing of adenosine based on novel photoluminescent vanadium nanoclusters anchored on MoS2 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Mohandoss, Sonaimuthu
Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Mishra, Kanchan
Tamargo, Ramuel John Inductivo
Palanisamy, Subramanian
Yelithao, Khamphone
You, SangGuan
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Highly selective and rapid sensing of adenosine (AdN) based on novel photoluminescent vanadium nanoclusters (VNCs) anchored on molybdenum disulfide nanosheets (MoS2-NS) are reported for the first time. The remarkably photoluminescent VNCs features excellent water solubility, ultra-small size, and bright photoluminescence. The VNCs on the MoS2-NS (MoS2-NS/VNCs) are well dispersed in aqueous solution and features a remarkable increase in bandgap energy (2.67 eV) compared to MoS2-NS (2.10 eV). The photoluminescence of VNCs was quenched by MoS2-NS via photoluminescence resonance energy transfer (PRET) mechanism. Furthermore, MoS2-NS/VNCs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity toward AdN over other AdN analogues, biomolecules, and metal ions. Consequently, the absorption spectral maxima appeared at 650 nm and emission significantly enhanced in intensity at 525 nm. Interestingly, the stronger binding of AdN with MoS2-NS/VNCs was fast with nearly 65.7 % photoluminescence restored in less than 5 min. The PL lifetime of MoS2-NS/VNCs (τ1 = 8.20 ns, τ2 = 0.68 ns) increased (τ1 = 10.48 ns, τ2 = 0.79 ns) in the presence of AdN. The observed limit of detection was 29 pM. This VNCs sensor system can be constructed as XNOR logic gate with MoS2 and AdN as chemical inputs by examining the mode of emission. Finally, this probe was used to detect AdN in live cells and human serum samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoclusters
MoS2-NS
Adenosine
Cancer cells
Serum samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PRET quenching mechanism of VNCs by MoS2-NS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Communication and Information Technology
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 , 2017R1C1B5076286 , and 2018R1A2B2004432 ), the Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ), and the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ).

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss is a Post-Doctoral Fellow (PDF) in the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. He received award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017–2018). His current research interest focuses on supramolecular chemistry, chemosensors, nanomaterials, computational chemistry and living cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis and PL excitation and emission spectra of VNCs and inset photographs under visible (left) and UV irradiation light at 365 nm (right); (b) PL spectra of VNCs as a function of excitation wavelength from 350 to 450 nm; (c) FTIR spectra of l-Cys (i) and VNCs (ii), (b) XPS spectrum of V2p (d) and S2p (e) regions of VNCs; (f) TEM images of the VNCs (inset; particle size diameter distribution), (g) HRTEM image of VNCs with lattice fringes.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of (i) MoS2-NS and (ii) MoS2-NS/VNCs (inset; photograph of exfoliated MoS2-NS suspension, a zoomed-in view of the data and Brillouin zone); (b) Tauc plots of (i) MoS2-NS and (ii) MoS2-NS/VNCs(c) PL quenching spectra of VNCs with the increasing concentrations of MoS2-NS ranging from (a–i) 0–80 μg/mL, respectively. (d–f) FTIR, XRD and Raman spectrum of (i) MoS2-NS and (ii) MoS2-NS/VNCs.
(a–c) FE-SEM images of MoS2-NS in a different image of the layered structure and (d–f) VNCs adsorbed on the surface of MoS2-NS.

(a,c) TEM images, (b,d) HRTEM images with lattice fringes of the as-prepared MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs, (e,f) Schematic atomic model representation of the MoS2 and V atoms attached to the MoS2 edge distances between the atoms.

(a,b) SAED pattern, (c,d) HAADF-STEM images of the MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs, (e,f) Schematic atomic model representation of the MoS2 and V atoms attached to the MoS2 edge showing similar contrast of the experimental HAADF-STEM image.

(a) UV–vis spectra of MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10−4 M) in the presence of AdN at various concentrations (a–l) 0–100 nM at PBS 7.4 buffer, (b) absorption intensity of MoS2-NS/VNCs at 650 nm versus AdN concentration, (c) Benesi–Hildebrand plot between relative absorption signal 1/(A−A0 ) and AdN concentrations, (d) Job’s plot evaluated from the absorption spectra of AdN (100 nM) with MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10-4 M) were mixed in varying ratios and the change in absorbance at 650 nm was measured in pH 7.4 buffer as function of molar ratio ([AdN]/([MoS2-NS/VNCs] + [AdN]).

(a) PL spectra of MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10−4 M) in the presence of AdN at various concentrations (a–l) 0–100 nM with PBS 7.4 buffer, (b) PL intensity of MoS2-NS/VNCs at 525 nm versus AdN concentration, (c) Benesi–Hildebrand plot between relative PL signal 1/(P−P0 ) and AdN concentrations, (d) Job’s plot evaluated from the absorption spectra of AdN (100 nM) with MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10-4 M) were mixed in varying ratios and the change in PL intensity at 525 nm was measured in pH 7.4 buffer as function of molar ratio ([AdN]/([MoS2-NS/VNCs] + [AdN]).

(a) Time-dependent PL intensity at 525 nm in MoS2-NS/VNCs using various concentrations of AdN at pH 7.4 in buffer solution, (b) Reversible changes in PL intensity of VNCs at 530/525 nm upon alternate addition of MoS2 (500 μM; 80 μg/mL) and AdN (100 nM) in pH 7.4 in buffer solution. (c) PL emission profile of VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs, VNCs/AdN, and MoS2-NS/VNCs@AdN (d) Truth table and schematic of an exclusive-NOR (XNOR) gate.

Fluorescence and bright-field images of MCF-7 cell incubated with (a) VNCs; 150 μg/mL, (b) MoS2-NS/VNCs (150 μg/mL) for 4 h and in the presence of AdN (c) 50 nM; 13.36 μg/mL and (d) 100 nM; 26.72 μg/mL for 15 min. (scale bar is 10 μm).
Schematic illustration of VNCs anchored on the MoS2-NS probe for PRET-based AdN detection.
Results of detection of AdN in a human serum sample with the proposed PL sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rapid response and highly selective sensing of adenosine based on novel photoluminescent vanadium nanoclusters anchored on MoS2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk-do, 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk-do 38541, Republic of Korea.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon, 25457, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly selective and rapid sensing of adenosine (AdN) based on novel photoluminescent vanadium nanoclusters (VNCs) anchored on molybdenum disulfide nanosheets (MoS2-NS) are reported for the first time. The remarkably photoluminescent VNCs features excellent water solubility, ultra-small size, and bright photoluminescence. The VNCs on the MoS2-NS (MoS2-NS/VNCs) are well dispersed in aqueous solution and features a remarkable increase in bandgap energy (2.67 eV) compared to MoS2-NS (2.10 eV). The photoluminescence of VNCs was quenched by MoS2-NS via photoluminescence resonance energy transfer (PRET) mechanism. Furthermore, MoS2-NS/VNCs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity toward AdN over other AdN analogues, biomolecules, and metal ions. Consequently, the absorption spectral maxima appeared at 650 nm and emission significantly enhanced in intensity at 525 nm. Interestingly, the stronger binding of AdN with MoS2-NS/VNCs was fast with nearly 65.7 % photoluminescence restored in less than 5 min. The PL lifetime of MoS2-NS/VNCs (τ1 = 8.20 ns, τ2 = 0.68 ns) increased (τ1 = 10.48 ns, τ2 = 0.79 ns) in the presence of AdN. The observed limit of detection was 29 pM. This VNCs sensor system can be constructed as XNOR logic gate with MoS2 and AdN as chemical inputs by examining the mode of emission. Finally, this probe was used to detect AdN in live cells and human serum samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is the most important cause of death globally, and responsible for approximately 10 million deaths in 2018 [1]. Nowadays, the majority of important health issues are caused by cancer, which is a complex disease caused by disruption in cellular metabolic processes [2]. For the last couple of years, adenosine (AdN) has attracted significant attention, owing to its considerable endogenous modulation, with different physiological functions in all organs, tissues, and cells, through diverse extrinsic and intrinsic signaling pathways [3]. The AdN-dependent pathway has predictive features and therapeutic targets for a variety of malignant cells such as colorectal, pancreatic, and breast cancer cells [4]. Abnormal AdN concentration levels have been strongly associated with neurodegeneration and heart failure [5]. Thus, sensitive detection of AdN is required to understand its physiological functions and action mechanisms especially with regards to cancer therapeutics. Recently, the development of sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity towards AdN have attracted much attention [6–8]. However, these methods require toxic heavy metals for the preparation of nanomaterials, sophisticated analytical instrumentations, complicated sample preparation and time-consuming procedures. Therefore, to overcome the aforementioned problems, it is essential to develop a straightforward, selective, and reliable method of detecting AdN.

As a prominent 2D material, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has received considerable attention as an emerging platform for biosensor applications. MoS2 consists of hexagonal layer of molybdenum with two covalently bonded sulfur atoms (S-Mo-S). More importantly, it has large indirect and direct band gaps of approximately 1.2 eV and 1.8 eV, respectively [9]. The MoS2 nanosheets (MoS2-NS) have stimulated tremendous interest due to their mechanical, optical, and electronic properties making them favorable materials for photothermal therapy, drug delivery, and bioimaging applications [10]. Because of its various crystallographic orientations and other features such as large surface area, ion absorptivity, and intercalation [11], MoS2-NS are known to possess excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility without acids or other oxidative reagents. Hence, these properties make it suitable as a nanostructure in biosensing applications [12]. Significantly, the MoS2 anchored with metal oxides [13], carbon nanomaterials [14], and noble metals [15], exhibits various improved characteristics owing to the synergistic effect. Because of their intrinsic nature, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) achieve notable interest [16] as photoluminescent probes in photoluminescence resonance energy transfer (PRET)-based sensors. Such sensing materials contain a quencher and fluorophore probe to create a PRET pair, where the biomolecular recognitions are coupled with the distance-dependent PL quenching. Hence, novel photoluminescent donor materials with high quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime, and excellent photo-stability are in high demand to control these hurdles. Up to date, there are no sensor studies that focus on the photoluminescent vanadium nanoclusters (VNCs). The transfer rate from MoS2 to the van der Waals material increases distinctly as the thickness of the nanomaterial decreases [17,18]. The efficient energy transfer PL quenching between the VNCs (zero-dimensional emitter) and the MoS2-NS (two-dimensional absorber) seems to be promising for optical detection.

Recently, studies on various fluorophore assays for AdN and its phosphate activity detection have gained much traction from researchers worldwide [19–33]. For example, Zhang and Guo's research group showed a fluorescent probe for AdN sensing with the use of DNA-templated MNCs or cationic conjugated polymers (CCP) as a signal indicator [34,35]. To our knowledge, the introduction of VNCs to MoS2-NS has not yet been demonstrated even more so its novel application as PRET-based platform for AdN detection. In this study, the introduction of AdN into the MoS2-NS/VNCs complex effectively results in an increased PL exhibited by the sensor (Scheme 1 ). Moreover, the linear response of the sensor with the introduction of AdN was demonstrated which earlier studies were not able to perform. The advantages of our synthesized probe can be seen in Table S1 which depicts the linear range of 0–100 nM (R2 = 0.9912) and the lowest detection limit (LOD) (29 pM) in comparison to reported methods for the detection of AdN. In general, our proposed protocol has the following features: (i) the synthesis and application of highly sensitive probe are rapid, facile, and straightforward; (ii) the materials used in this work do not pose high hazard and toxicity. The PL changes of VNCs upon the addition of MoS2 and AdN can be utilized as XNOR logic gate. Furthermore, the proposed method was evaluated for its cell viability and imaging effect in the MCF-7 cell line. Finally, the quantification feasibility of the sensor was used to detect AdN in serum samples with satisfactory results.

MoS2 nanosheets were synthesized through a mixed solvent sonicated-exfoliation method with necessary modifications [36]. Generally, MoS2 powder (0.15 g) was mixed in 50 mL of ethanol-water solution (45 %, v/v) as the dispersion solvent. The solution mixture was further sonicated at ambient temperature (25 °C) for 8 h, which resulted in the black bulk MoS2. Then, the dispersed and removed bulk MoS2-NS was centrifuged for 30 min at 10000 rpm followed by the collection of a faint yellowish-green supernatant. Afterwards, 1.0 mL of the MoS2 colloidal solution with 2.0 mL of 10 mM PBS 7.4 was centrifuged at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting MoS2-NS solution was immediately mixed with 100 mM of 100 μL VCl3 aqueous solution which was then added dropwise into the 10 mM of 2.0 mL l-Cys solution. Thereafter, 250 μL of NaOH solution (0.5 M) was added into the mixture with continuous stirring. After keeping the reaction for 30 min, the final product were collected from the resulting suspensions and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. Finally, the PL spectra of the samples were recorded with excitation at 390 nm.

MoS2-NS quenching was evaluated by measuring the PL of VNCs (3 μM final concentration) incubated with stock solutions at different concentrations of MoS2-NS (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 μg/mL) in PBS 7.4 buffer at 25 °C. The kinetics of VNCs adsorption was studied by adding MoS2 (500 μM; 80 μg/mL) solution containing VNCs (3 μM) in 10 mM PBS 7.4 buffer at 25 °C (MoS2-NS/VNCs, 5.03 × 10−4 M final concentration).

To assess the specificity of an improved sensor for AdN, the absorption and PL of MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10−4 M) with a series of compounds were examined. Typically, the aforementioned stock solutions were treated with the effects of other nucleosides (CtN, GuN, and UdN), biomolecules (GSH, DTT, MET, Glu, Fru, and Suc) and metal ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Ca2+) (1 μM) diluted with 2.0 mL of 10 mM PBS 7.4 buffer and shaken without interruption using a spinning mixer at room temperature for 5 min. To monitor the reaction time between MoS2-NS with VNCs (MoS2-NS/VNCs) and AdN, different solutions of MoS2 (500 μM; 80 μg/mL), VNCs (3 μM), and AdN (5–100 nM) were prepared. The resulting solution was diluted with 2.0 mL of 10 mM PBS 7.4 buffer and incubated up to 10 min at room temperature.

The cell viability effect of VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs, and MoS2-NS/VNCs in the presence of AdN was tested against MCF-7 cells using a WST-1 assay. Briefly, the cells at a concentration of 5 × 103 mL−1 were seeded in a 96 well plate at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, 100 μL of fresh culture medium were treated with various concentration of VNCs (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 μg mL−1), MoS2-NS (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg mL−1), MoS2-NS/VNCs (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μg mL−1), MoS2-NS/VNCs (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μg mL−1) in the presence of 50 nM (13.36 μg mL−1) and 100 nM (26.72 μg mL−1) of AdN (see Supporting Information for the concentration matrix) in 10 mM PBS 7.4 buffer. Over the incubation period, the cells were centrifuged for 5 min at 1000 rpm, followed by the addition of the WST-1 solution to each well. The cells were incubated again 4 h at 37 °C. The optical density (OD) was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (EL-800, Bio Tek Instruments, Winooski, VT USA). Furthermore, the morphological changes of VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs in the presence of AdN (50 and 100 nM) treated MCF-7 cancer cells were visualized under bright field and confocal laser scanning microscopic analysis (CLSM-710, Carl Zeiss, Germany). Consequently, the cells were washed with 1XPBS and stained with dual fluorescence dyes such as DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) and propidium iodide.

Human blood was collected from healthy volunteers in our laboratory. Usually, 1.5 mL of the blood sample was kept in a water bath at 37 °C for about 0.5 h. Then, 2.0 mL acetonitrile was added and centrifuged for 30 min at 5000 rpm to remove proteins and macromolecules. The supernatant was subsequently evaporated by keeping in a water bath (60 °C) for about 30 min. Finally, diluted serum samples (0.45, 0.50 and 0.55 μM) were spiked with the various known concentrations of AdN (0.1–1.0 μM), maintaining the physiological pH of the medium using PBS 7.4 buffer. Then MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10−4 M) were added and experiments were run after equilibration at room temperature for 5 min before the all experimental test. Each experiment was repeated 3 times with recovery and relative standard deviation values were used in the standard addition method.

The l-cysteine (l-Cys)-templated vanadium nanoclusters (VNCs), which demonstrated to emit strong photoluminescence (PL), were prepared according to the procedure with little modifications (see Supporting Information, Scheme S1) [37]. In aqueous VNCs solutions, the excitation and emission maxima originated from the VNCs rather than the l-Cys and VCl3 as shown in Figs. 1 a and S1, respectively. A colorless solution absorption peak was observed at 390 nm, but no other peaks were observed along the 300−800 nm region of the plasmon resonance peak and PL emission maxima centered at 530 nm. This indicates the formation of VNCs rather than the formation of vanadium nanoparticles (VNPs) [38]. The photograph in the inset of Fig. 1a shows the colorless solution of the VNCs under visible light and the strong green-emitting PL under ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm. Interestingly, the emission maxima exhibited a significant shift at the excitation wavelength in the range of 350 nm–450 nm (Fig. 1b), which indicates that the VNCs exhibited excitation-dependent PL properties [39]. Surface ligand plays a significant role in the optical properties of VNCs, especially for the quantum yields (QYs) and lifetime measurements. The PL quantum yields (QYs) of the green-emitting VNCs were calculated to be 20.92 %, while the QYs of other thiolated metal NCs rarely exceed 0.1 % [40]. The VNCs made the material an excellent electron donor with high QY. The decay curve of VNCs has a component of an estimated excited lifetime of τ1 (12.71 ns) and τ2 (8.90 ns) (Fig. S2). The details of QY, lifetime measurements and also optimization of VNCs such as salt concentration and reaction time conditions details are provided in the supporting information (Figs. S3 and S4).

The FTIR spectra of l-Cys exhibited numerous characteristics bands such as –SH stretching at 2532 cm−1 which disappeared in the spectrum of the nanoclusters. This suggests the existence of V atoms from VNCs through the formation of the VS bonds (Fig. 1c) [41]. The XPS survey spectrum of the VNCs (Fig. S5), the oxidation state of V, and the binding energy position of V2p3/2 and V2p1/2 located at 517.2 eV and 523.9 eV [42], indicate that the VNCs were composed of V(0) or V(I), respectively (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the two peaks at 162.4 eV and 164.8 eV indicate the characteristic signals of the S2p and VS bond assigned to the oxidation state of sulfur, respectively (Fig. 1e) [43]. Therefore, the shifting of the other observed VNCs peaks indicates the successful synthesis of VNCs (Fig. S6). The TEM images show that the VNCs have ultra-small particles with an average size of approximately 3 nm without significant aggregation or any visible VNPs formation (Fig. 1e). The HRTEM shows the VNCs as ultra-small spherical particles with excellent crystalline and lattice fringes of 0.23 nm corresponding to the (111) plane (Fig. 1f). The size of VNCs was calculated from the distribution histogram of the particle size and was found to be 3.0 ± 0.23 nm (n = 50) as shown in the inset of Fig. 1f. Based on these results, the VNCs synthesis was shown to be effective resulting to promising PL probe with excellent optical and morphological properties.

The UV–vis spectrum of the exfoliated MoS2-NS and in the presence of VNCs was recorded as shown in Fig. 2 a. The MoS2-NS exhibit major characteristic peaks, observed at 670 nm, 610 nm, 460 nm, and 215 nm, which were consistent with the MoS2-NS peaks acquired using the solution method shown in Fig. 2a (i) [44]. The inset of Fig. 2a shows a photograph of the MoS2-NS obtained with a yellowish-green suspension, which was more stable without precipitation. The MoS2-NS had two notable A and B excitonic peaks, which were observed at 670 nm and 610 nm, respectively, arising from the direct excitonic transitions at the large splitting of the K-point in the Brillouin zone (inset of Fig. 2a) [45]. With the VNCs anchored on the MoS2-NS, a new peak appeared at 260 nm along with an increase in intensity. Additionally, the MoS2-NS at 460 nm disappeared, which suggests the adsorption of VNCs on the MoS2-NS surface, as shown in Fig. 2a (ii). In the magnified view of the spectra (370−800 nm), it can be seen that the absorption edges of the MoS2-NS/VNCs largely shifted to a longer wavelength at 630 nm and 675 nm corresponding to lower band gap [46]. Fig. 2b shows the Tauc plots to determine the band gaps. The MoS2-NS made using VNCs exhibits a redshift, and the band gaps calculated from Tauc plots are observed longer wavelength at 2.10 eV and 2.67 eV for MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs (incorporation of V content), respectively. Our band gap data, values in the range 2.1–2.7 eV, are in good agreement with that previously reported vanadium anchored on nanomaterials [47,48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PRET quenching mechanism of VNCs by MoS2-NS
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to optimize the ratio between the quencher and donor molecules to set-up a PRET assay with high quenching efficiency [27]. To understand the exact mechanism of VNCs quenched by MoS2-NS (see Supporting Information), the following experiments were carried out. VNCs show high PL emission at 530 nm with the excitation of 390 nm strongly overlaps with the absorbance spectrum of MoS2-NS (Fig. S7). This results in the energy transfer from the VNCs to MoS2-NS effectively quenching the PL of VNCs [44]. The optimization of reaction conditions of pH plays a vital role in obtaining VNCs with bright and stable PL. The pH of the medium was maintained at 7.4 buffer. Herein, it is relevant to mention that the PL intensity of VNCs depends on the pH of the medium (Fig. S8). In pH range from 3 to 6, minimal change in PL intensity was observed. On the other hand, the emission intensity of VNCs dramatically enhanced upon increasing pH to 7 within the investigated pH range while increasing the pH above 8 was accompanied by a decrease in PL intensity. In this regard, the working pH value of the following experiments in this study is 7.4 in consideration of the natural physiological environment.

Meanwhile, Fig. 2c shows the PL quenching of VNCs which is dependent on the concentration of MoS2-NS (0–80 μg/mL). The PL signal of VNCs blue shifts to 525 nm and gradually decreases as the concentration of MoS2-NS increases from 0 to 80 μg/mL. During this process, the sharp decrease of VNCs PL indicates that the MoS2-NS/V nanocluster successfully prevented the recombination of generated electrons and holes [49]. Under these conditions, the VNCs can adsorb on the surface of MoS2-NS in close proximity via van der Waals force between nucleobases and the basal plane of MoS2-NS, resulting in PL quenching due to PRET [18]. The energy transfer efficiency is calculated using the equation, QE = (P0-Pq)/P0 , Where P0 is the original PL signal of the control group (VNCs solution), and Pq is the PL intensity of VNCs after quenching by MoS2-NS. The calculated quenching efficiency and normalized PL intensity (Pq/P0) are shown in Fig. S9a. When the MoS2 concentration was increased to 80 μg/mL−1, the PL intensity of VNCs was quenched to 2.4 % of the original PL response. However, no significant change in the photoluminescence signal has been observed when the concentration of MoS2-NS continued to increase (data not shown). As a result, 80 μg/mL−1 was taken as the optimized concentration for MoS2. Fig. S9b depicts well-dispersed and transparent solutions at the MoS2-NS concentrations lower than 80 μg/mL while dark and turbid mixtures are obtained with concentrations higher than 80 μg/mL (90 or 100 μg/mL).

Besides, the quenching kinetics behavior of MoS2-NS PL quenching was also investigated by monitoring the PL intensity as a function of time and the interaction between MoS2-NS and VNCs through π-π interactions. The results indicate that the PRET assay is a fast and efficient process where the equilibrium is reached within 2 min (Fig. S10) [49]. Herein, the MoS2-NS/VNCs composite seems to be effective by virtue of the overlapping absorption-emission spectral regions. The quantum yield (Φ) value was measured at 390 nm by following an integrated spherical model, which was found to be 0.38 %. To further demonstrate this hypothesis, we measured the lifetime measurements of VNCs in the absence and presence of MoS2-NS. As shown in Fig. S11, the most prominent thing observed in the quenching process is the reduction in the fluorescence lifetime by the addition of MoS2 NS to VNCs along with a consequent decrease in the fluorescence intensity. The original decay time i.e, τ1 = 12.71 ns, τ2 = 8.90 ns of VNCs decreased to τ1 = 8.20 ns, τ2 = 0.68 ns in the presence of MoS2-NS (80 μg/mL−1), respectively [50,51]. These results indicate that PRET contributed to the PL quenching mechanism of VNCs by MoS2-NS.

The FTIR spectra for the MoS2-NS exhibited a major characteristic peak at 481 cm−1, which is attributed to the Mo-S vibration, while the other peaks in the spectrum are most likely related to functional groups of solvent trapped in the MoS2-NS in Fig. 2d (i) [48]. The VNCs adsorbed on MoS2-NS, other peaks appeared at 3382 cm−1, 2877 to 2927 cm−1, and 1650 cm−1, which is attributed to the NH bond, CH stretching from CH3/CH2, and carboxylates of l-Cys in Fig. 2d (ii). The X-ray diffraction peaks for the MoS2-NS located at 15.6°, 35.4°, and 59.1°, corresponding to the (002), (100), and (110) planes [52], respectively, in Fig. 2e (i). This phenomenon suggests that the (002) peak diffraction was high and sharp, which indicates that the ultrathin MoS2-NS had a good crystalline structure and matches with the hexagonal phase of the MoS2 data (JCPDS No. 37-1492). The MoS2-NS/VNCs observed additional peaks at 43.2° and 61.3° corresponding to the (111) and (201) planes matching the standard JCPDS pattern of V (No. 36-1139) in Fig. 2e (ii) [53]. The major Raman E1 2g peak at 387.3 cm-1 is attributed to the out-of-plane motion while the A1g peak corresponding to the in-plane motion at 411.5 cm-1 is strongly connected with the MoS2-NS thickness in Fig. 2f (i). The MoS2-NS/VNCs exhibited the major peaks with a representative E1 2g shift at 387.6 cm-1 and A1g shift at 410.2 cm-1, which is close to that of the monolayer MoS2-NS [54]. Additionally, the new peak appeared at 1,100 cm-1 in Fig. 2f (ii) [55], and the intensities of both the E1 2g and A1g peaks decreased with the MoS2-NS/VNCs. The XPS survey scan, which reveals the presence of the Mo, S, V, O, N, and C elements, was further used to analyze the chemical states of the MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs (Fig. S12a). The Mo4+ oxidation state in the MoS2 energies (230.5 eV and 232.7 eV) are attributed to the doublet Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2, respectively, while the remaining peak at 226.3 eV corresponds well to the S2s lines of MoS2. The peaks at 163.2 eV and 161.7 eV in the S2p spectrum are attributed to the S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 orbits of the divalent sulfide (S2−) ligand oxidation state of sulfur (Fig. S12b,c) [56]. The V2p1/2 and V2p3/2 observed at 523.7 eV and 516.3 eV, respectively, revealed the oxidation state of V by following the previous report (Fig. S12d) [57]. The Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 were observed to shift at 228.1 eV and 232.2 eV, respectively, which can be readily attributed to the Mo4+ and other peaks existing in the MoS2-NS adsorbed V (Fig. S12e–i).

The FE-SEM revealed that a sheet-like MoS2 was stacked by a huge number of MoS2-NS in different directions (Fig. 3 a–c) [58]. The results indicate that the V nanoclusters (ultra-small size) were evenly coated on the MoS2-NS surface (Fig. 3d–f). The EDS and mapping analysis confirmed the presence of uniformly distributed elemental Mo, S, V, C, O, and N (Figs. S13 and S14). The stoichiometric ratio between the Mo:S and Mo:S:V was estimated from the quantitative analysis composition as 1:2 and 1:2:1, respectively [59]. In the TEM images, the exfoliated MoS2 appears as thin sheets with sizes varying from 100 to 200 nm shown in Fig. 4 a. The MoS2-NS surface exhibited dot-like particles decorated with thin nanosheets due to the presence of adsorbed VNCs (Fig. 4b) [59], which adequately match the FE-SEM observation. Additionally, the TEM image shows that many VNCs were adsorbed on the MoS2-NS surface with a size of ∼3 nm (Fig. S15). The width of the highly parallel lattice fringes was 0.23 nm and 0.62 nm; the particles d-spacing corresponding to the (002) and (111) planes of VNCs were adsorbed on the well-crystallized MoS2-NS (Fig. 4c,d) [60]. The schematic atomic model representation confirmed the lattice spacing of d = 0.62 nm and d = 0.23 nm (Fig. 4e,f), with the crystal face in the (002) and (111) planes of the MoS2 and VNCs. The SAED pattern of the MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs had a polycrystalline monolayer and the inter-planar (d) was reciprocal to the spacing between the diffraction spots and the center spot shown in Fig. 5 a,b [61]. Subsequently, the HAADF-STEM has been performed appearing in bright contrast, where the Mo atoms exhibited higher bright variation than the S atoms, and confirming the honeycomb structure of the stacking sequence in the MoS2 layers (Fig. 5c) [62]. The high-magnification view of the boxed area with the V atoms (magenta dots) at the S site showing the various V binding sites at the edge can be seen in Fig. 5d. Based on the schematic atomic models of the MoS2-NS and MoS2-NS/VNCs (Fig. 5e,f), multiple slice acquisition from the detector in the HAADF-STEM image simulation matched well with contrast due to the presence of Mo, S, and V, and the positions relative to the MoS2 lattice.

In this work, AdN sensor was designed by using MoS2-NS/VNCs as a signal marker for the AdN recognition unit. The UV–vis absorption peaks of MoS2-NS/VNCs (5.03 × 10−4 M) were observed at 260 nm and 650 nm, and AdN absorption intensity changes up to 100 nM (Fig. 6 a). In the absence of AdN, VNCs would be adsorbed on the surface of MoS2-NS via π–π interaction between the MoS2-NS and VNCs due to the sp2 hybrid electronic structure of MoS2-NS. As a result, the energy donor VNCs-AdN and the energy acceptor MoS2-NS were taken into proximity. The drastic increase in absorption signifies that the AdN concentration changes the structure of MoS2-NS/VNCs, wherein AdN interacts with the ground state [63]. The relative change in the absorption intensity at 650 nm with an increase in AdN concentration has been shown in inset Fig. 6b. A Benesi–Hildebrand plot of 1/[A-A 0] against 1/[AdN] shows good linearity (R2 = 0.9961), confirming the formation of MoS2-NS/VNCs@AdN (Fig. 6c). To elucidate the interaction between AdN and MoS2-NS/VNCs were mixed in varying ratios (see Supporting Information). By observing the change in absorbance at 650 nm, the binding stoichiometry was determined by Job plot experiment plotted as a function of molar ratio ([AdN]/([MoS2-NS/VNCs] + [AdN]) [64]. The maximum absorption band was reached at a molar fraction of 0.5, which demonstrated that the coordination ratio of the MoS2-NS/VNCs and AdN is at 1:1 stoichiometry (Fig. 6d).

The selectivity of the novel detection system toward target AdN was carried out. For this purpose, AdN analogues including CtN, GuN, UdN, and biomolecules of GSH, DTT, MET, Glu, Fru, Suc, and metal ions of Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+ and Ca2+ were evaluated at a concentration of 1.0 μM while the concentration of AdN was 100 nM. As shown in Fig. S18a,b, it was apparent that only AdN gave significant enhanced absorption and emission due to the formation of the complex structure indicating the superior selectivity for AdN [19–33]. Generally, thiol-containing compounds, such as l-Cys, can interfere with the enzyme activity in biological fluids. On the basis of their reactivity, l-Cys-capped VNCs are produced and significantly quench the PL of MoS2 system through strong interaction with the VNCs. Consequently, AdN can directly react with l-Cys to form photoluminescence without the addition of nucleophiles [66,67]. The thiol group of l-Cys acts as a nucleophile toward the intermediate imine. Because AdN is highly effective at inhibiting VNCs activity, the MoS2 probe combined with the MoS2-VNCs system can discriminate AdN from AdN analogues and its selectivity toward AdN is superior to that of other nucleoside-based sensors. The high selectivity of AdN can be explained by the inherent structure of AdN and the binding interaction between AdN and MoS2-VNCs substrate. In comparison to the complex protein matrices in human serum, AdN is a small molecule, and it can be more readily absorbed on the narrow gaps of nanomaterials. Also, due to the heterocyclic aromatic structure of AdN, the π–π interactions of MoS2-VNCs@AdN can serve as a strong driving force to bring AdN in close proximity to the binding sites of MoS2-VNCs. These provide the rationale for the ability of the system to discriminate AdN from its analogues, biomolecules, and metal ions. Consequently, the new peak in the absorption spectra appeared at 650 nm [72] and emission significantly enhanced at 525 nm [26,70] (Fig. S18a,b).

To further reveal that the MoS2-NS/VNCs has remarkable selectivity, interference studies were carried out to observe the PL response to AdN (100 nM) even in the presence of a higher concentration of various interference molecules (Fig. S19a,b) [26]. The presence of AdN at 100 nM resulted in a significant PL response while the other AdN analogues (10 μM) did not respond. No apparent interference was observed on biomolecules and metal ion at 10 μM despite having a concentration 10 times higher than that of AdN. For the selectivity and interference studies, the UV–vis and PL spectra response representing the change of the relative absorption and emission intensity of MoS2-NS/VNCs at 650 nm and 525 nm upon treatment with nucleosides, biomolecules, and metal ions are shown in Fig. S20a,b. Overall, these results reveal that this system exhibits excellent selectivity towards AdN.

The time response is a vital key to characterization for the PL sensor to detect AdN for practical application. To achieve rapid AdN detection using MoS2-NS/VNCs, we examined the time-dependent photoluminescent kinetics (Fig. 8 a). Upon adding AdN to MoS2-NS/VNCs, the PL intensity at 525 nm increased rapidly with time and achieved its maximum within 5 min. Approximately no change was observed with increased time up to 10 min, which indicated that MoS2-NS/VNCs were an effective PL probe able to detect AdN rapidly. Meanwhile, the reversibility of novel photoluminescent chemosensors is particularly an attractive feature for sensing applications. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the alternate additions of a constant concentration of MoS2-NS (500 μM; 80 μg/mL) and AdN (100 nM) to the VNCs solution resulted in the changes in the PL intensity. The reversibility of the PL response of VNCs anchored with MoS2-NS from quenched to the enhanced state was demonstrated by the further addition of AdN. The addition of the MoS2-NS to the VNCs complex shows that the titration of AdN is also reversible (turn-on-off-on), which could be repeated several times with little PL efficiency loss after the recovery of AdN. Moreover, the PL quantum yield and lifetime measurements of VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs@AdN were further confirmed for the reversibility studies also discussed in the above section.

Logic gates have been paid much attention by various researchers owing to their sensor application in molecular switch and keypad devices [73,74]. The binary output is denoted as ON (binary value, 1) or OFF (binary value, 0) of the PL of VNCs. As shown, the experimental results can be converted to a true value (Fig. 8c,d). After the addition of MoS2, PL intensity declined to a lower level for VNCs. Similarly, the alternate additions of AdN to VNCs with corresponding recovery exhibited a switchable change in the PL intensity. Addition of both inputs MoS2 and AdN results to the enhancement of PL emission which makes the output of gate remains high as 1. Such changes in PL upon the actions of two chemical inputs copied the performance XNOR logic gates [75] (Fig. 8d).

In vitro cytotoxicity and biocompatibility experiments were performed on non-cancerous mouse fibroblast cells (3T3-L1) and MCF-7 cancer cells [76]. The cell viability and biocompatibility studies of 3T3-L1 treated with MoS2-NS/VNCs in the presence of AdN are shown in Figs. S21,22 (see Supporting Information). MCF-7 cells treated with VNCs, MoS2-NS, MoS2-NS/VNCs, and MoS2-NS/VNCs in the presence of AdN were examined at different concentrations for 24 h (Fig. S23) [77]. Images of MCF-7 cancer cells treated with VNCs, MoS2-NS/VNCs, and MoS2-NS/VNCs in the presence of AdN were obtained using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis (Fig. 9 ). Distinct changes were observed in the photoluminescence response showing dead cancer cells generated from the PI staining. After incubation with VNCs at 37 °C for 4 h, the cells exhibited intense PL (turn-on) in the red channel which demonstrated that VNCs is cell-permeable (Fig. 9a). The results of the PL sensitivity studies agrees with the earlier observed photoluminescence quenching in the VNCs anchored on MoS2-NS surface (MoS2-NS/VNCs) [78]. Besides, a very weak PL signal was found in MCF-7 cancer cells treated with probe VNCs anchored on MoS2-NS surface (MoS2-NS/VNCs) (Fig. 9b), owing to the PL quenching caused by PRET assay system [79]. Upon addition of AdN (50 nM) with MoS2-NS/VNCs, treated MCF-7 cancer cells exhibited intense fluorescence (Fig. 9c), whereas the intensity of the MoS2-NS/VNCs@AdN PL emission showed an increase. In contrast, Fig. 9d, the MCF-7 cancer cells exposed to AdN (100 nM) with MoS2-NS/VNCs at 37 °C for 15 min displayed intensified enhanced fluorescence [80] which is consistent with the PL experiment (vide supra). While the intensity difference is visually subtle, the PL measurements indicate higher intensities with higher AdN concentration. Moreover, more photoluminescent cells can be observed in Fig. 9d. Furthermore, the normal shape of the cells in bright field images (Fig. 9) confirmed that the cells were viable during the imaging experiments. Owing to its low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, the probes could be effective and beneficial for various biological applications including expressed cancer cells.

The developed sensor was used as a photoluminescent probe for the determination of AdN in human serum samples. The serum samples were diluted 10 times with phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). The experiment was repeated 3 times with each sample obtained results are presented in Table 1 . The corresponding recoveries for AdN in serum samples were in the range of 97.2–101.3 % with very less RSD ranging from 1.9 to 3.0%, indicating that the assay has acceptable levels of accuracy and precision [19–33]. The experimental results demonstrate that the MoS2-NS/VNCs-based sensor has the potential to monitor AdN in complex biological matrixes.

In this study, adenosine nanosensor in human serum samples has been developed using novel vanadium nanoclusters anchored on molybdenum nanosheets which are reported for the first time. The quenching mechanism via PRET has been confirmed through comprehensive series of studies, namely TEM, PL spectroscopy, kinetic quenching, quantum yield, and fluorescence lifetime experiments. The molybdenum nanosheets have been used as a good photoluminescence quencher to significantly suppress the background signal and improve the detection sensitivity. The vanadium nanoclusters anchored on molybdenum nanosheets are strategically used to turn-off the while its discriminative behavior toward the adenosine results to photoluminescence restoration (turn-on). The detection limit was shown to be as low as 29 pM. The photoluminescence changes of VNCs upon the addition of MoS2 and AdN can be utilized as XNOR logic gate. The satisfactory results of detecting adenosine in biological samples show its promising future in clinical application. The proposed synthetic strategy has numerous attractive features, including low cost, ease of preparation, rapid turn-on response, high quantum yield, long lifetime, low cytotoxicity, and high serum recovery.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345, 2017R1C1B5076286, and 2018R1A2B2004432), the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189), and the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675).

S. Zhang, Q. Niu, L. Lan, T. Li, Sensors Actuators, B Chem. 240 (2017) 793–800. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.054.
T. Lin, L. Zhong, L. Guo, F. Fu, G. Chen, Nanoscale. 6 (2014) 11856–11862. doi:10.1039/c4nr03393k.
V. Bojinov,. Chem. Technol. Metall. 46 (2011) 3–26. http://dl.uctm.edu/journal/node/j2011-1/1_Vlado_Bojinov.pdf.
F. Wang, Z. Wang, Q. Wang, H. Kwon, P.J. Jeon, J.S. Kim, T.-Y. Kim, H. Yun, S.W. Lee, T. Lee, S. Im, 2D Mater. 3 (2016) 044001. doi:10.1088/2053-1583/3/4/044001.
S. Mohandoss, J. Sivakamavalli, B. Vaseeharan, T. Stalin, Sensors Actuators, B Chem. 234 (2016) 300–315. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.148.
L. Sucha, M. Hroch, M. Rezacova, E. Rudolf, R. Havelek, L. Sispera, J. Cmielova, R. Kohlerova, A. Bezrouk, P. Tomsik, Oncol. Rep. 30 (2013) 2593–2602. doi:10.3892/or.2013.2778.
Q. Zhao, J. Shi, J. Feng, K. Li, Y. Chen, H. Chen, M. Ma, S. Wang, Biomaterials. 39 (2014) 206–217. doi:10.1016/j.biomaterials.2014.11.009.
G. Oudeng, M. Au, J. Shi, C. Wen, M. Yang, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 10 (2018) 350–360. doi:10.1021/acsami.7b18102.
M.S. Feiz, A. Meshkini, Int. J. Biol. Macromol. 129 (2019) 1090–1102. doi:10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.08.158.
N. Dale, A. V. Gourine, E. Llaudet, D. Bulmer, T. Thomas, K. Michael Spyer, J. Physiol. 544 (2002) 149–160. doi:10.1113/jphysiol.2002.024158.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sonaimuthu Mohandoss is a Post-Doctoral Fellow (PDF) in the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. He received award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017–2018). His current research interest focuses on supramolecular chemistry, chemosensors, nanomaterials, computational chemistry and living cells.

Raji Atchudan was born in Dharmapuri, Tamil Nadu, India, in 1979. He received his B.Sc., (2000) in Chemistry from University of Madras; M.Sc., (2003) in Chemistry from Annamalai University; M.Phil., (2004) in Chemistry from University of Madras; and Ph.D. (2011) in Chemistry (Nanoscience and Nanotechnology) from Anna University, India. He had worked at Kyungpook National University, Republic of Korea as a Postdoctoral Research Fellow with Prof. Jin Joo (2012–2015). At present (2015–till date), he is working as a Foreign Research Professor in the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests include facile green synthesis of metal oxide nanoparticles, carbon nanostructured materials-carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, graphene oxide/graphene and carbon-related nanomaterials, and their application towards the photocatalytic degradation of anthropogenic dyes, electro-catalyst, supercapacitors, sensors, and bioimaging.

Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison was born in Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India, in 1984. He received his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees in Chemistry from Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, in 2004 and 2008, respectively; and Ph.D degree in Chemistry from Gandhigram Rural Institute-DU, Gandhigram, Tamil Nadu, India, in 2014. Since 2015, he has been working as a Foreign Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. His research interests include green synthesis of metal nanoparticles, semiconductors and carbon nanoparticles and utilizing these materials as catalyst, electro-catalyst, supercapacitors, batteries and sensors.

Kanchan Mishra is a postdoctoral associate at School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University. He completed his PhD on February 22, 2018 from the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University. He is doing research on sustainable and green synthesis of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles for the organic transformations. His research interests include nanocatalyst, mechanistic study, theoretical investigation, green synthesis, recyclable and reusable catalyst.

Ramuel John Inductivo Tamargo was born in Makati, Philippines in 1991. He received his BS degree in Chemical Engineering from the University of the Philippines-Diliman in 2014. Since 2015, he has been working as a joint MS-PhD research student under the guidance of Prof. Yong Rok Lee at the Organic Materials Synthesis Laboratory, School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. His current research interest includes the development of new methodologies for the synthesis of organic materials with interesting properties for biological, optical, and sensing applications.

Subramanian Palanisamy is a Post-Doctoral Fellow (PDF) in the Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, South Korea. He had completed his M.Sc and Ph.D degree in the Department of Animal Health and Management, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, Tamil Nadu, India. During his doctoral and pre-doctoral research, he has worked in polysaccharides, nanomaterials and its biological activities. His current research interest includes Polysaccharides, Nanomaterials and its pharmacological activities.

Khamphone Yelithao is a Ph.D. student in the Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, South Korea. He received his Master degree in this same university in 2017. He has four-year teaching experience in Souphanouvong University as a Lecturer. His research interest mainly focused on the separation of marine bio-active materials and analysis their immunomodulatory and cancer properties.

SangGuan You is currently working as a Professor in the Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, College of Life Science, South Korea. He received his Bachelor and Master degrees at Gangneung National University and Korea University in the year of 1997 and 1999 respectively. He completed his Doctoral studies in the field of Food Engineering, University of Manitoba, Canada. Also, he had worked as a Senior Researcher at the University of Manitoba (2005). He is a member of the American Association of Cereal Chemist and editorial board member of Bioactive carbohydrates and dietary fiber. His current research focuses on the structural analysis of polymers and its pharmaceutical activities such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anticancer and immunomodulatory.

Yong Rok Lee is a Chunma Distinguished Professor of the School of Chemical Engineering at Yeungnam University, South Korea. He received his B.S. degree in Chemistry from Chonbuk National University in 1982, M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Organic Chemistry from Seoul National University in 1984 and 1992, respectively, under the supervision of Prof. Eun Lee, all in South Korea. He had worked at Duke University as a postdoctoral fellow with Prof. M. C. Pirrung (1993–1994), Ohio State University as a postdoctoral fellow with Prof. L. Paquette (1995), and Michigan State University as a visiting professor with Prof. W. Wulff (2000). His current research focuses on the synthesis and applications of organic materials, metal nanomaterials, natural products, and pharmaceuticals.
